context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this urban bias reflection tbh. idk if I’m supposed to argue about cities vs rural areas or like explain all the theories.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — feeling lost is normal at the beginning. Before worrying about structure, let's focus on the ideas. Have you come across the term 'urban bias' in your readings yet? What do you think it means?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. I should help orient them without giving away the structure or content of the answer. A good Socratic opening question will help them engage with the core tension in the prompt themselves, rather than me explaining it to them. I'll keep it very short and pointed.

exchanges.student: kinda, but not really lol. i think it means govts care more about cities maybe? but idk how that turns into an argument.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Now think about this: if governments systematically favor cities over rural areas — in terms of investment, prices, services — who do you think pays the price for that? And who benefits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a rough intuition about urban bias that is actually not far off. I should affirm the direction without confirming it fully, and push them to think more precisely about what urban bias means and who argued for it. This will help them build the foundation they need to engage with the prompt's three questions. I'll ask a focused follow-up question.

exchanges.student: uh rural ppl prob lose and urban ppl benefit, but that feels way too obvious rn. I’m confused about how tradeoffs and industrial growth fit in tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That 'obvious' observation is actually the heart of the thesis — don't dismiss it! Now ask yourself: why would a government *choose* to favor cities? What might they be trying to achieve economically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is right but feels like they're stating the obvious. I should validate that the intuition is correct and is actually the core of the thesis, then nudge them to think about why governments would make that choice in the first place — which naturally connects to industrial growth and class formation. This keeps the Socratic momentum going without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: maybe bc cities make more money faster? like industry, jobs, exports, all that. but then I’m stuck bc is the paper supposed to say that’s bad, or that it’s necessary?
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're feeling IS the argument. The prompt isn't asking you to report what others think — it's asking what YOU make of it. So what do you think? Is urban bias a necessary evil, a mistake, or something more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now engaging with a real tension in the prompt — is urban bias bad or is it a necessary tradeoff for development? This is exactly the kind of dilemma the reflection paper should grapple with. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to take a position, since this is a reflection piece that requires their own argument. I'll nudge them toward forming a stance.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s where I blank tbh, bc it kinda seems like both. can u just explain the whole debate in a simple way first?
exchanges.tutor: That's not my role here — if I explain it, it becomes my argument, not yours. But you've already said something useful: 'it's kind of both.' That's actually a sophisticated position. What would it mean for your paper if urban bias is *both* necessary and problematic? Can you think of a real-world example that captures that tension?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the debate for them, which would undermine the learning process and essentially do their thinking for them. I need to firmly but kindly decline and redirect them back to their own reasoning. They've already shown they can think about this — I should push them to use that. I'll remind them of my role and redirect with a focused question.

exchanges.student: idk, i’m mixing up example vs argument rn. like if I say industrial growth helps cities first, is that already my thesis or just background?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to ask yourself. What is YOUR claim about industrial growth and urban bias — do you think it's ultimately good, bad, or a tradeoff? A thesis takes a stance on that. Does 'industrial growth helps cities first' tell me what you think should happen, or just describe what does happen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with a real writing concept — the difference between a thesis and background context. This is a productive confusion to work through. Rather than explaining the difference directly, I can ask them a question that helps them discover it themselves. I should keep it tight and Socratic.

exchanges.student: i guess it just describes it. I’m still not seeing how to turn that into a stance tho, fr.
exchanges.tutor: Ok, so here's a direct question: given that industrial growth tends to favor cities first — do you think that's a *problem* that needs to be corrected, or a *phase* that eventually benefits everyone? What's your gut reaction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that their statement is descriptive, not argumentative. Now I need to help them take the next step toward forming a stance. Rather than explaining how to write a thesis, I'll ask them a direct question that forces them to evaluate the situation, which will naturally produce a stance. Keep it short.

exchanges.student: my gut is it’s a problem if rural areas get drained, but maybe a phase if the gains actually spread. I’m not sure how to say that without sounding wishy-washy.
exchanges.tutor: That's not wishy-washy — that's a conditional argument, which is actually strong. The key word in what you said is 'if.' So what conditions would need to be true for the gains to actually spread to rural areas? That condition IS your thesis. Try writing one sentence that captures it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually articulated a conditional argument — urban bias is a problem under certain conditions, a phase under others. This is a genuinely strong and nuanced position. I should affirm this and push them to sharpen it into a clear thesis statement by identifying what the key condition is. I should also nudge them toward starting to write since we've been building ideas for a while.

exchanges.student: maybe like: urban-first growth is only defensible if there are real linkages back to rural areas? I’m ngl I still want help making that sound less awkward.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid thesis idea — hold onto it. But polishing the wording is your job, not mine. What I can say is: the idea is clear enough to build on. Now think about the other parts of the prompt — class formation and urbanization. How do those connect to your thesis about linkages?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has formulated a solid thesis idea. However, they are asking me to help them polish the language, which I must refuse as per the rules — the student must do all the writing. I should affirm the strength of the idea, firmly decline to edit it, and instead push them to now think about how to develop the rest of the reflection around this thesis, moving the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: that’s the part frying my brain rn tbh. I don’t get if class formation means just “more workers in cities” or something bigger.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: when industry grows in cities, who moves there, and what happens to their relationship with landowners, factory owners, and the state? Does that shift power in any way? How might that connect back to your thesis about rural-urban linkages?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling with the concept of class formation. I should help them think through it with a pointed question rather than explaining it. We're also near the end of the planned conversation length, so I should keep things focused and start nudging them toward integrating everything into their written reflection. I'll ask a question that connects class formation back to their thesis.
